text: The surgeon demonstrates the Renne stopper repair of an incisional hernia located three centimeters below the umbilicus. The contents of the hernia sac are confirmed to be omentum, with no bowel present, allowing for monopolar dissection of thick fibrous strands. The fibrous strands are dissected sharply, revealing fat that is gently manipulated and reduced. Counter pressure is applied externally to aid in the reduction of the omentum in a clockwise manner. Remaining fibrous strands are identified and divided using monopolar cautery. The entire omentum content is reduced, and the sac is inspected to ensure no remnant omentum is left, preventing postoperative complications. A small defect through the primary defect is noted.

The surgeon makes a small incision in the preperitoneal layer, raising a flap approximately seven centimeters above the apical portion of the defect. The dissection reveals a plane suitable for incisional hernia repair, demonstrating entry into the retrorectus space. A small buttonhole is visible on the posterior rectus sheath, indicating deeper dissection towards the muscle, following the proper Stopa technique for mesh placement.

The surgeon divides the peritoneum and posterior rectus sheath around the hernia defect, revealing the transversalis fascia and entering the preperitoneal plane. The preperitoneal pad of fat is dissected, exposing the rectus muscle and small fibrous fatty structures, which are divided. The inferior epigastric artery is visible above the instrument as the dissection extends laterally beyond the rectus muscle. A loop is brought through a stab incision on the lower aspect of the defect for primary closure.

The surgeon takes bites on either side of the edge of the defect in the anterior abdominal wall, focusing on suturing the linea alba without involving excessive thickness of the rectus abdominis muscle. Manipulation from the outside aids in taking these bites, ensuring a good thickness of the edge is included. The thread is elongated as suturing progresses to maintain adequate length for closure.

The surgeon retrieves one limb of the Vicryl suture attached to a 15x15 cm lightweight macroporous polypropylene mesh. Two central trans-facial sutures are placed in the midline to anchor the mesh anteriorly using intracorporeal sutures. The mesh is secured with absorbable 2-0 Vicryl sutures, avoiding lateral trans-facial sutures to prevent nerve entrapment and chronic pain. Sutures are placed loosely to avoid muscle necrosis and tension. The upper left corner of the mesh is anchored to the muscle, and the mesh is neatly positioned on the anterior abdominal wall. The defect created by dissection around the hernia is sutured using Vicryl.

The surgeon completes the suturing of the raised flap using a running suture technique, ensuring the flap is securely closed without any buttonhole defects. The mesh covers a wide area, restoring the physiology of the abdominal wall.
